old_claim.note: Art Conservation Workshops provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Human Anatomy & Physiology better matches the source-backed academy curriculum of anatomy lectures and figure drawing; art conservation workshops are not required by this institutional teaching scope.

source_shape.source_locator: First art academies in Renaissance Italy section: the article says students at the 1563 Florence academy learned the arts of drawing and heard lectures on anatomy and geometry.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source-backed instructional curriculum for early public art academies includes drawing, anatomy, and geometry rather than conservation workshops.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The replacement edge is justified because the cited curriculum explicitly names anatomy lectures inside the academy, while conservation workshops are not part of the node's first-academy teaching claim.

ontology_before: Public Art Academies depended on Art Conservation Workshops, implying conservation practice enabled formal art-academy instruction.
ontology_after: Public Art Academies depends on Human Anatomy & Physiology and Perspective in Art, matching the teaching scope of drawing, anatomy, geometry, and artistic theory.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: public_art_academies now depends on human_anatomy_physiology and no longer directly depends on art_conservation_workshops.
would_reject_if: A stronger source shows the 1563 academy was organized around conservation workshops rather than instruction in drawing, anatomy, and geometry.
The node is narrowed to modern conservation academies instead of Renaissance public art instruction.
human_anatomy_physiology is removed without adding a more specific anatomy-for-art-training prerequisite.
short_reason: Anatomy is documented in the academy curriculum; conservation workshops are not.
